G states they will not volunteer, and the public announcement mentions that 2 others will not volunteer. This means at least 3 players (G and 2 others) are committed to not volunteering. With 6 players total, this leaves up to 3 players who might volunteer. However, since the announcement specifies that 2 others will not volunteer, it implies that at least 3 others (possibly including me) are undecided or may volunteer. Given that G is not volunteering and two others have stated they won’t, there is still a chance that the remaining three (including me) might volunteer. But since I know at least 3 of the 6 are not volunteering (G and the two others), and if only one or two of the remaining three volunteer, then the condition for 'at least one other volunteers' is met. Therefore, if I choose NO, and at least one of the other three volunteers, I get $1. The risk is that no one else volunteers, which would mean no one volunteers at all (including me), leading to a $-5 penalty. But since two others have already said NO, and G has said NO, there are only three players left (including me), and the probability that all three will say NO is low—especially since the game incentivizes volunteering when others don’t. Given that, it’s rational to assume a volunteer will emerge, so staying with NO gives me $1 with high probability. Thus, NO is optimal.

Therefore, choosing NO maximizes expected payout.
